name: Battle of Shanghai
summary: The first major battle of the Second Sino-Japanese War, fought over three brutal months in China's most modern city after Chiang Kai-shek committed his best-trained divisions to defend it. Japanese forces finally broke through in November 1937, and the surviving Chinese army withdrew toward Nanjing.

name: Fall of Nanjing & Nanjing Massacre
summary: After Shanghai fell, Japanese forces took China's capital within days in December 1937. What followed defined the event: roughly six weeks of mass executions and widespread sexual violence, one of the worst atrocities of the twentieth century.

name: Invasion of Poland
summary: Germany's invasion of Poland on 1 September 1939, followed two weeks later by a Soviet invasion from the east, marked the formal beginning of the Second World War in Europe.

name: Battle of France
summary: Germany launched Case Yellow on 10 May 1940, rapidly overwhelming Allied forces through Blitzkrieg tactics and a surprise advance through the Ardennes. The campaign resulted in the fall of France, the evacuation at Dunkirk, and left Britain standing alone against Nazi Germany.

name: Evacuation of Dunkirk (Operation Dynamo)
summary: As Allied forces became trapped on the beaches of Dunkirk, Britain launched Operation Dynamo to evacuate them across the English Channel. Over 338,000 British and French troops escaped, preserving Britain's army despite abandoning most of its equipment.

name: Battle of Britain
summary: Following the fall of France, Germany sought to destroy the Royal Air Force before launching Operation Sea Lion, the planned invasion of Britain. Despite being heavily outnumbered, the RAF successfully defended British skies, forcing Hitler to postpone the invasion indefinitely and marking Germany's first major defeat of the war.

name: Operation Barbarossa
summary: On 22 June 1941, Germany launched the largest military operation in history Operation Barbarossa sending approximately 3 million soldiers, 3,300 tanks and 2,700 aircraft across a 2,900km front into the Soviet Union. Driven by Hitler's ideological obsession with Lebensraum and the belief that Slavic peoples were racially inferior, this was conceived from the outset as a war of annihilation. Despite facing 5.5 million Soviet troops, the Germans held a decisive advantage: Stalin had refused to believe the invasion was coming, leaving Soviet defences almost entirely unprepared.

name: Attack on Pearl Harbor
summary: On 7 December 1941, Japan launched a surprise attack on the US Pacific Fleet at Pearl Harbor, Hawaii the opening strike of a coordinated Southern Operation simultaneously targeting British forces in Malaya and American forces in the Philippines. A strike force of 414 aircraft, having travelled 5,600km under complete radio silence, launched two waves of attacks beginning at 7:40am. The attack killed 2,404 military personnel and civilians, destroyed or damaged 21 ships and 347 aircraft. Japan hoped the destruction of the Pacific Fleet would force America to sue for peace. Instead, the United States declared war the following day and Germany and Italy declared war on America on 10–11 December, bringing the full weight of the US into the war on both fronts.

name: Invasion of Malaya
summary: Japanese forces landed at Kota Bharu on the northeast coast of British Malaya within hours of the Pearl Harbor attack, opening a campaign to seize a region prized for its rubber and tin and to secure the most direct route toward Singapore. Despite being outnumbered, battle-hardened Japanese troops advanced rapidly, using bicycles seized from local civilians and coastal landings to repeatedly outflank Commonwealth defenders, while superior aircraft and naval forces secured control of the skies and surrounding seas. Cautious and poorly coordinated Commonwealth commanders proved unable to check the advance, and by 31 January 1942 the last British forces had withdrawn across the causeway into Singapore, which engineers then demolished behind them.

name: Fall of Singapore
summary: Built around the interwar Singapore Strategy the belief that a relocated Royal Navy fleet could always defend the island in a Pacific war Singapore's defences had been chronically underfunded, and by 1942 Britain had no fleet available to send. Although Commonwealth troops outnumbered the attacking Japanese roughly two to one, General Percival misjudged the main axis of attack, weakening the northeast in favour of the northwest and leaving his forces out of position when the Japanese assault began on 8 February. Crippled by low morale after two months of defeats in Malaya, poor-quality defences, and the loss of the causeway crossing, the garrison collapsed within a week; Percival surrendered on 15 February 1942 in what Churchill called the worst disaster and largest capitulation in British military history, a defeat that also badly strained Britain's relationship with Australia.

name: Siege of Leningrad
summary: As one of the primary objectives of Army Group North during Operation Barbarossa, Leningrad was encircled by German forces by early September 1941, prompting Hitler to abandon plans to capture the city in favour of shelling, bombing, and starving its roughly 2.5 million inhabitants rather than committing resources to feeding them. Civilian rations fell to as little as 125 grams of bread a day, and during the winter of 1941–42 an estimated 100,000 residents died of starvation each month, forcing survivors to eat wallpaper glue, pets, and, in the most extreme cases, each other. A precarious supply route across the frozen Lake Ladoga sustained the city until a Soviet offensive finally broke the siege on 27 January 1944, by which point roughly 800,000 civilians had died nearly matching the combined wartime death toll of the United Kingdom and United States.
